lessons.title: Hydrographic Survey Fundamentals
learning_objectives: Define hydrographic surveying and state its principal purposes and deliverables.
Distinguish the major acoustic sensing methods: single-beam, multibeam, and side-scan sonar.
Explain how horizontal position and vertical depth are combined into a reduced sounding.
key_takeaways: Hydrography measures the bottom and features of water bodies and reduces them to a chart datum.
Single-beam gives a profile, multibeam gives full-coverage bathymetry, side-scan gives an acoustic image.
Every sounding needs a horizontal position, a measured depth, and a water-level correction to a datum.

content.html:
What hydrographic surveying is

Hydrographic surveying is the branch of surveying that measures and describes the physical features of water bodies and the land beneath them: depths, the shape of the bottom (bathymetry), the shoreline, tides and currents, and submerged hazards. Its classic purposes are safe navigation (nautical charts), engineering (dredging, channels, pipelines, bridge and dam siting), resource and environmental work (sediment volumes, habitat, scour), and boundary determination along water. Unlike a topographic survey on dry land, the surveyor cannot see or directly occupy the surface being mapped, so depths are sensed remotely from a moving vessel and the water surface itself is constantly changing height. A hydrographic survey therefore always couples three measurements at every point: a horizontal position, a measured depth, and a water-level (tide) observation used to reduce that depth to a fixed vertical datum. The reduced result is a depth referenced to a chart datum that anyone can re-use, regardless of the tide stage at the moment of measurement.

Acoustic sensing methods

Modern depths are measured acoustically with an echosounder, which times an acoustic pulse from the transducer to the bottom and back. Three sensor families dominate:

  • Single-beam echosounder (SBES): one narrow vertical beam giving a depth directly under the vessel. Cheap and simple, it yields a profile of soundings along the track line; coverage between lines is interpolated.
  • Multibeam echosounder (MBES): a fan of many beams across-track, producing a dense swath of soundings on each pass and full-bottom coverage. It is the standard for charting, channel surveys, and volumetric work.
  • Side-scan sonar (SSS): tows or mounts transducers that insonify the bottom obliquely to produce a photo-like acoustic image. Side-scan excels at finding objects (wrecks, debris, rock, pipelines) and characterizing bottom texture, but does not by itself give accurate depths.

In practice a charting survey often pairs MBES for bathymetry with SSS for object detection, while a simple cross-section job may use only SBES.

content.label: Acoustic depth from two-way travel time (c = sound speed, t = travel time)

content.text: Acoustic depths depend on the speed of sound in water (~1500 m/s), which varies with temperature, salinity, and pressure. A sound-velocity profile (SVP) is collected and applied so beams are ray-traced correctly; an uncorrected SVP biases outer multibeam beams the most.

content.html:
From raw echo to charted depth

A raw sounding is only the distance from the transducer to the bottom at the instant of measurement. To be useful it must be reduced: corrected for the transducer draft (how deep the transducer sits below the waterline), for vessel motion (heave, roll, pitch from an inertial sensor), and most importantly for the stage of the tide. Because the water surface rises and falls, the same spot on the bottom would yield different raw depths at different times. By subtracting the observed height of the water surface above the chart datum, every sounding is referred to one common vertical datum (commonly Mean Lower Low Water for charts). The horizontal position of each sounding is fixed by GNSS, and the whole stream of position + depth + tide is time-tagged so the three can be merged. Lesson 2 covers tidal datums; Lesson 3 covers the survey methods and depth reduction in detail.

content.alt: Vessel with single-beam, multibeam, and side-scan sonar coverage patterns
content.caption: Figure: Coverage geometry of single-beam (point), multibeam (swath), and side-scan (oblique image) sensors.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Hydrography maps depths, bottom shape, shoreline, and submerged hazards from a moving vessel.
SBES = profile; MBES = full-coverage swath; SSS = acoustic image for object detection.
Depth = (sound speed x travel time) / 2, then reduced for draft, motion, and tide.
Every charted sounding combines a GNSS position, a measured depth, and a water-level correction to a datum.

flashcards.definition: A survey that measures depths, bottom configuration (bathymetry), shoreline, tides/currents, and submerged hazards of a water body, reduced to a chart datum.
An instrument that measures depth by timing an acoustic pulse from a transducer to the bottom and back; depth = (sound speed x two-way travel time) / 2.
An echosounder that emits a fan of beams across-track to produce a continuous swath of soundings, giving full-bottom coverage in one pass.
A sonar that insonifies the bottom obliquely to produce a photo-like acoustic image, used to detect objects and characterize bottom texture; it does not give accurate depths by itself.

lessons.title: Tidal Datums and Water Level Measurement
learning_objectives: Define the principal tidal datums: MLLW, MLW, MTL/MSL, MHW, and MHHW.
Explain tidal datum determination over the 18.6-year National Tidal Datum Epoch.
Compute tide range and relate observed water level to a chart datum.
key_takeaways: Tidal datums are long-term averages of high/low waters over a 19-year epoch, not single observations.
MLLW is the usual chart (depth) datum; MHW/MHHW are the usual boundary datums.
MHHW averages only the higher daily high water and lies landward of MHW.

content.html:
Why tidal datums matter

A tidal datum is a vertical reference plane defined by a long-term average of a particular phase of the tide. Because tides rise and fall continuously, a single water-level reading is useless as a reference; instead we average the same phase (e.g., every low water) over many years to get a stable plane. Two jobs depend on these datums. First, depth reduction: charted depths are referenced to a low-water datum so a mariner reading the chart sees a conservative (shallow) depth. Second, boundary determination: the legal edge of tidal water is defined by a high-water datum. Getting the datum wrong moves both the charted depth and the property line. The U.S. determines datums over the National Tidal Datum Epoch, a specific 19-year period (~18.6 years is the lunar nodal cycle) chosen so that periodic astronomical variations average out.

rows: Mean Higher High Water - average of the higher of the two daily high waters
Coastal boundary (esp. Spanish/Mexican grants in TX)
Mean High Water - average of all high waters
Mean Tide Level / Mean Sea Level - mean of MHW and MLW / mean of all levels
Mean Low Water - average of all low waters
Mean Lower Low Water - average of the lower of the two daily low waters

content.html:
Determining a datum at a site

A first-order tide station observes water level continuously for the full 19-year epoch and averages each phase directly. Most survey sites cannot wait 19 years, so a temporary gauge is set and observed for a shorter span (often a month or more), then tied to a nearby control tide station with a known accepted epoch datum. By simultaneous comparison (the difference or amplitude-ratio method), the short series at the project gauge is corrected to the equivalent 19-year value. The accepted datums are then connected to land by leveling from the gauge zero to permanent tidal bench marks, so the water-level datum and the geodetic vertical datum (e.g., NAVD88) can be inter-related. This bench-mark tie is what lets a GNSS or leveled elevation be converted into a height above MLLW or MHW.

content.text: Texas differs: the coastal boundary datum depends on the sovereign source of the grant. Spanish/Mexican grants are bounded by MEAN HIGHER HIGH WATER (MHHW); Anglo-American (Republic/State of Texas) grants are bounded by MEAN HIGH TIDE / MEAN HIGH WATER (MHW). Luttes v. State, 159 Tex. 500, 324 S.W.2d 167 (1959).

content.html:
Reducing an observed level to a datum

During a survey the gauge records the instantaneous water surface. To reduce a sounding, the surveyor needs the height of that surface above the chart datum at the moment each depth was measured. If the gauge zero is set on the chart datum, the recorded staff reading is exactly the tide height to subtract from the raw sounding. If the gauge zero is offset, the known offset is applied. Smoothly varying corrections (interpolated between high and low waters, or modeled) avoid step changes between soundings. The same reduced framework supports computing range statistics: for example, with MHW = 1.8 ft and MLW = 0.2 ft above datum, the mean tide range is 1.6 ft. These relationships are the basis for the lesson's tide-range and depth-reduction problems.

content.alt: Stacked tidal datum planes from MLLW up to MHHW with ranges labeled
content.caption: Figure: Tidal datum ladder (MLLW, MLW, MTL, MHW, MHHW) with mean and diurnal ranges.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Datums are 19-year (National Tidal Datum Epoch) averages of a tide phase, not single readings.
MLLW is the U.S. chart datum; MHW and MHHW are the usual boundary datums.
Short observations are corrected to the epoch by simultaneous comparison with a control station.
Tide range = MHW - MLW; great diurnal range = MHHW - MLLW.
Texas: MHHW for Spanish/Mexican grants, MHW for Texas-law grants (Luttes).

flashcards.definition: The average height of the lower of the two daily low waters over the National Tidal Datum Epoch; the standard chart (sounding) datum in the United States.
The average of the higher of the two daily high waters over the epoch; lies landward of MHW and is the Texas boundary datum for Spanish/Mexican grants.
A specific 19-year period (capturing the ~18.6-year lunar nodal cycle) over which tidal datums are averaged so astronomical variations cancel out.
The vertical difference between Mean High Water and Mean Low Water (MHW - MLW).

lessons.title: Bathymetric Survey Methods
learning_objectives: Plan sounding lines and density for single-beam and multibeam bathymetric surveys.
Apply depth reduction: reduced depth = raw sounding - tide height relative to datum.
Position the vessel and soundings with RTK GNSS and account for transducer offsets.
key_takeaways: Reduced depth = raw sounding - tide height above datum.
RTK GNSS provides centimeter horizontal positions and can supply the vertical reference too.
Bathymetric models (surfaces, contours, TINs) are built from reduced, positioned soundings.

content.html:
Planning the survey

A bathymetric survey produces a model of the bottom from many positioned, reduced soundings. Planning starts with line layout. For single-beam work, parallel sounding lines are run at a spacing set by the required detail (e.g., 25-100 ft for engineering surveys), with perpendicular check lines (cross lines) to test consistency where they intersect main lines. For multibeam, line spacing is set so adjacent swaths overlap (commonly ~25-50% in deeper water) to guarantee full coverage and to compare overlap. The surveyor also fixes the vertical control (which tidal or geodetic datum), the sound-velocity strategy, and the positioning method before going afloat. Good geometry plus controlled speed keeps sounding density uniform and avoids gaps.

Positioning the vessel with RTK GNSS

Each sounding needs an accurate horizontal position the instant the depth is sensed. The modern standard is Real-Time Kinematic (RTK) GNSS: a base receiver on a known control point (or an RTN/VRS network) broadcasts corrections to the vessel rover, giving centimeter-level horizontal positions in real time. The GNSS antenna, the echosounder transducer, and the motion sensor are rarely at the same point, so the surveyor measures the lever-arm offsets between them and applies them, together with the vessel's heading and attitude (heave, roll, pitch), so each sounding is referred to the actual transducer location and projected to the bottom correctly. RTK tide (ellipsoidally referenced surveying) can even replace a physical tide gauge: the RTK-derived antenna height, minus a geoid/separation model, yields the water-surface height relative to the chart datum directly.

content.label: Depth reduction: reduced depth = raw sounding - tide height above datum

content.text: Sign convention: depths are positive downward and tide height is positive above the chart datum. When the water is HIGH (large positive tide height) the raw echo is larger, so subtracting the tide height brings every sounding back to the same datum. A negative tide (below datum) increases the reduced depth.

rows: Observed tide height above chart datum

content.html:
From soundings to a bathymetric map

Once every sounding has a horizontal position and a reduced depth, the data are cleaned (spikes and noise removed), checked against the cross lines, and gridded into a bottom surface. From that surface the deliverables follow: depth contours, color-shaded relief, a TIN or DEM, cross-sections, and volumes (e.g., dredge cut/fill between a survey and a design grade). Quality is judged by agreement at main-line/cross-line intersections and by adherence to standards such as IHO survey orders. Because the map is only as good as the datum and positioning behind it, the surveyor documents the chart datum, the GNSS/RTK setup, sound-velocity handling, and the reduction applied so the bathymetry can be trusted and re-used.

content.alt: Plan view of parallel sounding lines with perpendicular cross-check lines over a channel
content.caption: Figure: Sounding-line plan with main lines and perpendicular cross (check) lines.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Plan line spacing and cross lines (single-beam) or swath overlap (multibeam) for full, checkable coverage.
RTK GNSS gives centimeter horizontal positions; lever-arm offsets tie GNSS, transducer, and motion sensor.
Reduced depth = raw sounding - tide height above datum.
Reduced, positioned soundings are gridded into surfaces, contours, and volumes; cross lines verify quality.

flashcards.definition: The raw, draft-corrected sounding minus the observed tide height above the chart datum, giving a depth referenced to that fixed datum.
Real-Time Kinematic GNSS: a base/rover (or network) setup providing centimeter-level real-time positions used to position the survey vessel and each sounding.
A sounding line run perpendicular to the main lines so that depth agreement at the intersections can be used to assess survey quality.
flashcards.hint_1: Crosses the main lines for QC.

flashcards.definition: The measured 3-D vector between the GNSS antenna, the transducer, and the motion sensor, applied so soundings refer to the true transducer location.

lessons.title: Coastal Boundary Determination
learning_objectives: Identify the tidal datum that defines a coastal upland boundary.
Apply the Texas dual standard from Luttes v. State (MHHW vs. MHW) based on sovereign source.
Distinguish accretion, reliction, erosion, and avulsion and their effect on a coastal boundary.
key_takeaways: A tidal coastal boundary is a tidal datum line surveyed on the ground, not a visible feature.
Texas is dual: MHHW for Spanish/Mexican grants, MHW for Texas-law grants (Luttes).
Gradual change moves the boundary (accretion/erosion); sudden avulsion fixes it.

content.html:
The boundary is a datum, not a line you can see

Along tidal water the upland (private) boundary is the intersection of a defined tidal datum plane with the sloping shore. The general American rule (Borax Consolidated v. City of Los Angeles, 296 U.S. 10 (1935)) places that line at Mean High Water (MHW) - the average of all high waters over the 19-year epoch. To survey it, you do not chase a wet/dry line on the sand; you establish the MHW elevation from a tide station, then locate where that elevation meets the beach profile (by leveling or RTK), tracing the contour landward and seaward along the shore. The result is reproducible because it is tied to an averaged datum, not to today's waterline.

content.text: Texas differs - the dual standard. The coastal datum depends on the original SOVEREIGN: Spanish/Mexican grants are bounded by MEAN HIGHER HIGH WATER (MHHW); Anglo-American (Republic/State of Texas) grants are bounded by MEAN HIGH TIDE / MEAN HIGH WATER (MHW). Luttes v. State, 159 Tex. 500, 324 S.W.2d 167 (1959). A coastal surveyor must establish the chain of title back to the original sovereign BEFORE choosing the datum.

content.html:
Why Texas is different: Luttes v. State

Luttes v. State (1959) concerned the 1829 Mexican "Potrero de Buena Vista" grant on the Laguna Madre. The Texas Supreme Court held that the seaward boundary of a Spanish or Mexican grant is the mean higher high water line, because the civil law in force when those grants issued defined the "shore" by the reach of the highest (winter) tides, which the Court translated into the surveyable MHHW datum. Grants made under Anglo-American law (after Texas's own sovereignty) take the ordinary mean high tide / MHW line. Because MHHW averages only the higher of each day's two high waters, the MHHW line sits landward of the MHW line - so the State's seabed is larger under a Spanish/Mexican grant. The practical consequence: identical-looking coastal tracts can have boundaries at different elevations purely because of their sovereign origin.

Movable boundaries: accretion, reliction, erosion, avulsion

Water boundaries move with the water - but only when the change is gradual. Accretion (slow build-up of alluvion) and reliction (slow uncovering as water recedes) move the boundary outward and the upland owner gains land. Erosion (gradual loss) moves it landward and the owner loses land. Avulsion - a sudden, perceptible change such as a hurricane or a flood cutting a new channel - is different: the boundary is FIXED at its former location and does not follow the new waterline. Title does not shift merely because the water suddenly jumped. This gradual-moves / avulsive-fixes framework is mainstream American law and Texas follows it; the Texas twist appears in beach-easement law (next lesson) and in that the line being measured on streams is the gradient boundary rather than a simple high-water mark.

sections.title: Case: Luttes v. State (1959)
sections.content: Seaward boundary of an 1829 Mexican grant on the Laguna Madre is mean higher high water, distinguished from the mean high tide line used for grants under Anglo-American (Texas) law. The controlling Texas authority on coastal upland boundaries; requires the surveyor to identify the sovereign source first.

sections.title: Contrast: Borax Consol. v. City of Los Angeles (1935)
sections.content: The general U.S. rule fixes the ordinary tidal boundary at mean high water (average of all high waters over the epoch). Texas applies this only to its own-law grants; it adds the landward MHHW line for civil-law (Spanish/Mexican) grants.

content.alt: Beach profile showing MHW line seaward of the landward MHHW line
content.caption: Figure: On a sloping shore the MHHW datum line lies landward of the MHW line.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Survey a tidal boundary by establishing the datum elevation and locating its intersection with the shore profile.
General rule = MHW (Borax). Texas dual rule: MHHW for Spanish/Mexican grants, MHW for Texas grants (Luttes).
MHHW lies landward of MHW, enlarging the State's seabed for civil-law grants.
Accretion/reliction and erosion move the boundary; avulsion fixes it at its former location.

flashcards.definition: Texas Supreme Court case holding that Spanish/Mexican coastal grants are bounded by mean higher high water (MHHW), while Anglo-American/Texas grants take the mean high tide (MHW) line.
The gradual, imperceptible deposit of soil (alluvion) along a water boundary; the boundary moves with the water and the upland owner gains the new land.
A sudden, perceptible change in a watercourse or shoreline (e.g., hurricane, channel cut); the boundary is fixed at its former location and title does not follow the water.
The ordinary tidal upland boundary (Borax, 1935) at the average of all high waters; the datum used in Texas for grants made under Anglo-American/Texas law.

lessons.title: Navigable Waterways and Riparian Surveys
learning_objectives: Apply the Texas statutory 30-foot rule for navigable streams.
Locate the boundary of a navigable Texas stream using the gradient boundary method.
Distinguish riparian (stream) from littoral (sea/lake) situations and State bed ownership.
key_takeaways: Texas defines a navigable stream by an average bed width of 30 feet 'from its mouth up.'
Navigable Texas streams have State-owned beds; the private boundary is the gradient boundary.
On non-navigable streams the abutting owner takes title to the center of the channel.

content.html:
Riparian vs. littoral, and why navigability matters

Owners along flowing water (a river or stream) hold riparian rights; owners along a sea or lake hold littoral rights. For a stream, the controlling question for the boundary and for who owns the bed is whether the stream is navigable. If it is navigable, the State of Texas owns the bed in trust for the public and the private patent stops at the stream boundary; if it is non-navigable, the abutting owner takes title to the center of the channel. So before running a riparian survey, the surveyor must decide navigability under Texas's tests, then locate the correct boundary line.

content.text: Texas differs - the 30-foot rule. A Texas stream is navigable-in-fact if it 'retains an average width of 30 feet from its mouth up.' The 30 feet is measured across the ENTIRE BED (bank to bank), not the water flowing on a given day - so a sometimes-dry channel can still be navigable, with a State-owned bed. Tex. Nat. Res. Code Sec. 21.001(3). Texas also recognizes navigability-in-law for streams actually usable for public passage.

content.html:
The Texas gradient boundary method

For a navigable Texas stream the line between State-owned bed and private upland is the gradient boundary - a distinctively Texas construction, not the ordinary high-water mark used in most states. Following Motl v. Boyd (1926) and Oklahoma v. Texas (1923), the method locates a "water-washed and relatively permanent" line on the cut bank and constructs the boundary along the gradient of the flowing water midway between the lower level of the grass on the bank and the lower level of the bed at its average and mean stage during a year - disregarding extreme freshets and droughts. The bed is the soil "alternately covered and left bare" as the water reaches its average and mean stage. Surveyor A.A. Stiles developed the computation for the Red River line. In practice the surveyor levels the bank profile, identifies the qualifying water-washed feature, and computes the gradient line - a more involved procedure than reading a single high-water mark.

sections.title: Case: Motl v. Boyd (1926)
sections.content: Texas Supreme Court adopted the gradient boundary between public and private ownership along navigable streams and defined the "bed" as the soil alternately covered and left bare, adequate to contain the water at its average and mean stage during an entire year, without reference to extreme freshets or droughts.

sections.title: Case: Oklahoma v. Texas (1923)
sections.content: The U.S. Supreme Court adopted the gradient boundary for the Texas-Oklahoma line on the Red River. Its commissioner-surveyor A.A. Stiles developed the gradient boundary method ('water washed and relatively permanent elevation ... at the outer line of the river bed').

sections.title: State ownership of the bed
sections.content: The State of Texas owns the beds of navigable streams in trust for the public. Private patents abutting a navigable stream run only to the gradient boundary; the bed below is sovereign land (subject to the Small Bill's partial relinquishment of the bed surface, but not minerals).

content.text: Texas differs - surface water is State-owned. Owning the streambank does NOT give a right to use the water beyond limited domestic and livestock use; appropriative use requires a State permit administered by TCEQ on a prior-appropriation basis. Tex. Water Code Ch. 11 (Sec. 11.021). Recognized riparian claims were merged into the permit system by the Water Rights Adjudication Act of 1967.

content.alt: Cross-section of a stream channel showing cut bank, bed, and the constructed gradient boundary line
content.caption: Figure: Gradient boundary constructed on the cut bank of a navigable Texas stream.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Riparian = streams; littoral = sea/lake. Navigability controls bed ownership and the boundary.
Texas 30-foot rule: average bed width of 30 ft from its mouth up makes a stream navigable (entire bed, not the water).
Navigable stream boundary = gradient boundary (Motl v. Boyd, Oklahoma v. Texas); State owns the bed.
Non-navigable stream = owner takes to the center; Texas surface water is State-owned (use needs a permit).

flashcards.definition: Texas statutory test: a stream is navigable if it retains an average bed width of 30 feet from its mouth up, measured across the entire bed (Tex. Nat. Res. Code Sec. 21.001(3)).
The distinctively Texas line between State-owned bed and private upland on a navigable stream, constructed along the gradient of the cut bank at the water's average and mean stage (Motl v. Boyd).
Riparian rights belong to owners along flowing watercourses (rivers/streams); littoral rights belong to owners along seas, bays, or lakes.

flashcards.term: State ownership of navigable stream beds
flashcards.definition: Texas owns the beds of navigable streams in trust for the public; abutting patents run only to the gradient boundary, with the bed below remaining sovereign land.
flashcards.hint_1: Public bed below the gradient line.

lessons.title: Environmental and Regulatory Considerations
learning_objectives: Identify the public-beach easement and rolling-easement doctrine under the Texas Open Beaches Act.
Explain how Severance v. Patterson limited the rolling easement after avulsion.
List key regulatory players and permits affecting coastal and hydrographic work.
key_takeaways: The Texas Open Beaches Act protects a public easement to the vegetation line on Gulf beaches.
Severance v. Patterson: the easement may roll with gradual change but not automatically after avulsion.
Coastal work intersects multiple agencies and permits (TCEQ, GLO, USACE, public-trust lands).

content.html:
The public beach and the Open Beaches Act

Texas law strongly protects public access to Gulf beaches. Under the Texas Open Beaches Act (TOBA), the public holds an easement over the dry beach, generally landward to the line of vegetation. Because shorelines move, that easement is treated as a rolling easement - as the beach naturally shifts, the public easement can migrate with it, so the boundary of public access is dynamic rather than a fixed survey line. For a coastal surveyor this means the relevant lines (the tidal boundary seaward and the vegetation line landward) are subject to change, and the work product should document the date and method of any shoreline location.

content.text: Texas differs - Severance v. Patterson, 370 S.W.3d 705 (Tex. 2012). The public beach easement may GRADUALLY migrate with naturally shifting beaches, but it does NOT automatically 'roll' onto previously private dry land after an AVULSIVE event (e.g., a hurricane suddenly moving the vegetation line landward). After avulsion, the State must establish a NEW easement rather than rely on the prior one rolling inland.

content.html:
Why the avulsion distinction matters

Severance v. Patterson imported the classic accretion/avulsion boundary principle into beach-access law. The result is a gradual / avulsive distinction applied to the easement itself: gradual shoreline change lets the public easement roll inland, but a sudden, avulsive change (a hurricane shifting the vegetation line) fixes the easement rather than rolling it onto land that was private before the event. This mirrors the rule that gradual processes move a water boundary while avulsion fixes it. For the surveyor, it means a single storm can change whether a strip of land is burdened by the public easement, and re-establishing the line after a major event may require a new legal determination rather than simply re-running the prior easement landward.

Regulatory players and permits

Coastal and hydrographic projects touch several jurisdictions at once. Surface water (every river, stream, bay, and arm of the Gulf) is State-owned; appropriative use requires a permit from the Texas Commission on Environmental Quality (TCEQ) under the prior-appropriation system (Tex. Water Code Ch. 11). The Texas General Land Office (GLO) manages State-owned submerged lands and coastal public-trust lands and holds the original grant records a coastal boundary survey must trace. Federal work in navigable waters and wetlands typically involves the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (Clean Water Act Sec. 404 / Rivers and Harbors Act permits) and may require environmental review. Dredging, beachfront construction, and shoreline alteration commonly need multiple approvals. The surveyor's role is to deliver defensible boundary and bathymetric data - tied to the correct datum and the correct sovereign chain of title - on which these permitting and ownership decisions rest.

rows: Permits appropriative use of State-owned surface water (prior appropriation)
Manages State submerged/public-trust lands; custodian of original grant records
Public beach easement to the vegetation line; rolls gradually, not after avulsion
Federal permits for work in navigable waters and wetlands (Sec. 404 / RHA)

content.alt: Beach cross-section showing tidal boundary, dry beach public easement, and vegetation line
content.caption: Figure: Public beach easement between the tidal boundary and the line of vegetation under TOBA.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: TOBA gives the public a rolling easement over the dry beach to the line of vegetation.
Severance v. Patterson (2012): the easement rolls with gradual change but not automatically after avulsion.
Texas surface water is State-owned; appropriative use needs a TCEQ permit (prior appropriation).
Coastal work also engages the GLO (public-trust lands, grant records) and USACE (federal navigable-waters permits).

flashcards.definition: Texas statute protecting a public easement over the dry beach, generally landward to the line of vegetation, on Gulf beaches.
flashcards.hint_1: Public access to the dry beach.

flashcards.definition: The doctrine that the public beach easement migrates with a naturally shifting beach, so the access boundary is dynamic rather than a fixed survey line.
Texas Supreme Court case holding the public beach easement may migrate with gradual change but does not automatically roll onto previously private land after an avulsive event.
Authorization from the Texas Commission on Environmental Quality to appropriate State-owned surface water under the prior-appropriation system (Tex. Water Code Ch. 11).
flashcards.hint_1: State owns the water; permit to use it.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which sensor produces a full-coverage swath of soundings across-track in a single pass?
quiz_questions.explanation: A multibeam echosounder emits a fan of beams across-track, giving full-bottom coverage per pass. Single-beam yields only a nadir profile; side-scan produces an image, not accurate depths; a lead line is a single manual depth.

quiz_questions.question_text: Side-scan sonar is primarily used to obtain accurate water depths.
quiz_questions.explanation: Side-scan produces a photo-like acoustic image used to detect objects and characterize the bottom; it does not by itself yield accurate depths. Depths come from echosounders.

quiz_questions.question_text: An echosounder measures a two-way acoustic travel time of 0.020 s in water where the speed of sound is 1500 m/s. What is the depth?
quiz_questions.explanation: Depth = (c x t)/2 = (1500 x 0.020)/2 = 30/2 = 15 m. The division by two accounts for the down-and-back path.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the standard chart (sounding) datum for U.S. nautical charts?
quiz_questions.explanation: U.S. charts reference depths to MLLW, a conservative low-water datum so mariners see shallow (safe) depths. MHW/MHHW are high-water boundary datums; MSL is a mid-level reference.

quiz_questions.question_text: The National Tidal Datum Epoch is a single high-water observation used to set datums.
quiz_questions.explanation: The epoch is a specific 19-year period over which a tide phase is averaged, capturing the ~18.6-year lunar nodal cycle so astronomical variations cancel out. It is not a single observation.

quiz_questions.question_text: At a station MHW = 1.9 ft and MLW = 0.3 ft above chart datum. What is the mean tide range?
quiz_questions.explanation: Mean tide range = MHW - MLW = 1.9 - 0.3 = 1.6 ft.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is a short tide-gauge record at a project site corrected to the full 19-year datum?
options: By averaging only the project gauge readings
By simultaneous comparison with a nearby control tide station of known epoch datum
By using a single spring-tide observation
quiz_questions.correct_answer: By simultaneous comparison with a nearby control tide station of known epoch datum
quiz_questions.explanation: Short series are reduced to the 19-year equivalent by simultaneous comparison (difference/ratio) with a control station whose accepted epoch datums are known. Short-record averages alone are not epoch datums.

quiz_questions.question_text: A raw, draft-corrected sounding is 16.40 ft when the observed tide height is 2.70 ft above chart datum. What is the reduced depth?
quiz_questions.explanation: Reduced depth = raw sounding - tide height = 16.40 - 2.70 = 13.70 ft. Subtracting the tide refers the depth to the chart datum.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the primary purpose of running cross (check) lines in a single-beam bathymetric survey?
options: To assess data quality by comparing depths where they intersect the main lines
To replace the need for a tide gauge
quiz_questions.correct_answer: To assess data quality by comparing depths where they intersect the main lines
quiz_questions.explanation: Cross lines run perpendicular to main lines so depth agreement at intersections provides a quality check. They do not set speed, replace tides, or measure sound velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why must lever-arm offsets between the GNSS antenna, transducer, and motion sensor be measured and applied?
options: Because the sensors are rarely co-located, so soundings must be referred to the true transducer position
Because GNSS does not work over water
Because the chart datum changes hourly
Because side-scan needs them for imaging
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Because the sensors are rarely co-located, so soundings must be referred to the true transducer position
quiz_questions.explanation: The antenna, transducer, and motion sensor sit at different points on the vessel; their 3-D offsets plus attitude must be applied so each sounding refers to the actual transducer location and bottom point.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under Luttes v. State, what tidal datum bounds the seaward edge of a Spanish or Mexican coastal grant in Texas?
options: Mean High Water / Mean High Tide (MHW)
quiz_questions.explanation: Luttes v. State (1959) holds that Spanish/Mexican grants are bounded by MHHW, reflecting civil-law definition of the shore by the highest tides. Texas-law grants use the MHW line.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a sloping shore, the MHHW line lies seaward of the MHW line.
quiz_questions.explanation: MHHW is a higher datum than MHW, so on a sloping shore the MHHW contour lies LANDWARD of MHW, enlarging the State's seabed for civil-law grants.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hurricane suddenly cuts a new channel and shifts the shoreline. How does this avulsive change affect the property boundary?
options: The boundary moves with the new waterline
The boundary is fixed at its former location
Title passes to the State automatically
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The boundary is fixed at its former location
quiz_questions.explanation: Avulsion (sudden, perceptible change) fixes the boundary at its former location; title does not follow the water. Only gradual accretion/erosion moves the boundary.

quiz_questions.question_text: Before choosing the tidal datum for a Texas coastal boundary survey, what must the surveyor first establish?
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The original sovereign source of the grant (Spanish/Mexican vs. Anglo-American/Texas)
quiz_questions.explanation: Because the datum is MHHW for Spanish/Mexican grants and MHW for Texas-law grants (Luttes), the surveyor must trace the chain of title back to the original sovereign before selecting the datum.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under the Texas statutory test, a stream is navigable if it retains an average width of how much, measured from its mouth up?
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas's 30-foot rule (Nat. Res. Code Sec. 21.001(3)) makes a stream navigable if it averages 30 feet of bed width from its mouth up - measured across the entire bed, not the flowing water.

quiz_questions.question_text: The 30-foot navigability measurement is taken on the width of water actually flowing on the day of the survey.
quiz_questions.explanation: The 30 feet is measured across the entire bed (bank to bank), not the water flowing on a given day, so even a sometimes-dry channel can qualify as navigable.

quiz_questions.question_text: What line forms the boundary between the State-owned bed and private upland along a navigable Texas stream?
options: The mean lower low water line
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas uses the distinctive gradient boundary (Motl v. Boyd; Oklahoma v. Texas), not the ordinary high-water mark. The center/thread applies to non-navigable streams; MLLW is a chart datum.

quiz_questions.question_text: In Texas, owning the bank of a stream automatically gives the owner the right to appropriate the water for irrigation.
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas surface water is State-owned; appropriative use requires a TCEQ permit under prior appropriation (Water Code Ch. 11). Bank ownership permits only limited domestic and livestock use.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under the Texas Open Beaches Act, the public beach easement generally extends landward to which feature?
options: The mean lower low water line
quiz_questions.explanation: TOBA protects a public easement over the dry beach to the line of vegetation. MLLW is a chart datum; the seawall and 30-foot line are unrelated to the beach easement.

quiz_questions.question_text: How did Severance v. Patterson (2012) limit the rolling beach easement?
options: It abolished the public beach easement entirely
It held the easement migrates with gradual change but does not automatically roll onto previously private land after avulsion
It fixed the easement permanently at the 1959 shoreline
It extended the easement to mean lower low water
quiz_questions.correct_answer: It held the easement migrates with gradual change but does not automatically roll onto previously private land after avulsion
quiz_questions.explanation: Severance applied the gradual/avulsive distinction to the easement itself: it can roll with gradual shoreline change, but after an avulsive event the State must establish a new easement rather than roll onto previously private land.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which Texas agency issues permits to appropriate State-owned surface water?
options: The Texas Commission on Environmental Quality (TCEQ)
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The Texas Commission on Environmental Quality (TCEQ)
quiz_questions.explanation: TCEQ administers appropriative surface-water permits under prior appropriation (Water Code Ch. 11). The GLO manages submerged/public-trust lands and grant records; USACE handles federal navigable-waters permits.

problem_templates.name: Reduced depth from raw sounding and tide height
problem_templates.question_template: A draft-corrected raw sounding of {{raw:f2}} ft is recorded when the observed tide height is {{tide:f2}} ft above the chart datum. What is the reduced depth (ft) relative to the datum?
problem_templates.explanation_template: Subtracting the tide height above datum ({{tide:f2}} ft) from the raw sounding ({{raw:f2}} ft) refers the depth to the chart datum: {{_answer}} ft.

images_needed.caption: Raw sounding, tide height above datum, and reduced depth

problem_templates.name: Mean tide range from MHW and MLW datums
problem_templates.question_template: At a tide station the Mean High Water datum is {{mhw:f2}} ft and the Mean Low Water datum is {{mlw:f2}} ft above chart datum. What is the mean tide range (ft)?
problem_templates.explanation_template: The mean tide range is the vertical difference between the MHW and MLW datums: {{mhw:f2}} - {{mlw:f2}} = {{_answer}} ft.

images_needed.caption: MHW and MLW datum planes with mean range labeled

problem_templates.name: Area of accreted land along a frontage
problem_templates.question_template: A riparian tract has {{frontage:f1}} ft of water frontage. Over a period of years, gradual accretion adds an average width of {{accr:f1}} ft of new land along the entire frontage. What is the approximate area of accreted land (square feet)?

solution_steps_template.title: Area formula
solution_steps_template.formula: area = frontage x average accretion width

problem_templates.explanation_template: Gradual accretion adds land to the upland owner. Treating the new land as a strip, area = frontage x average accretion width = {{frontage:f1}} x {{accr:f1}} = {{_answer}} sq ft.

images_needed.caption: Accreted strip of average width added along a frontage
